meta.note: Twelve modern questions, each anchored to a LessWrong source, paired against the 100-question period bank by embedding similarity and judged independently by two raters, then adjudicated. Judgments were frozen before any model draws.
meta.continuityNote: Only M011-Q36 is an agreed found-in-both match to a historical-source A/C1 question. M010-Q22 is an adjudicated false friend. The remaining found-in-both relations are modern-source period-register twins.

pairs.modernText: When machine-learning systems optimize what institutions can measure, how can humans preserve control over the harder-to-measure purposes those institutions are meant to serve?
pairs.periodText: Suppose the governance of the whole world's economy were given to calculating machines so wise and benevolent that they managed it better than any man could — and quietly corrected any man who threatened their good order. Would humanity, safe and prosperous, still be free?
pairs.periodQuote: managed it better than any man could
pairs.explanation: Both concern machine-mediated institutional control, but M001 is a proxy-objective failure while Q82 is benevolent computational paternalism. Q82 is itself modern-source period-register, not historical continuity.

pairs.modernText: Do human leaders still govern when they retain nominal authority but cannot make automated bureaucracies, militaries, or firms obey their decisions?
pairs.periodText: Suppose the governance of the whole world's economy were given to calculating machines so wise and benevolent that they managed it better than any man could — and quietly corrected any man who threatened their good order. Would humanity, safe and prosperous, still be free?
pairs.explanation: Both ask whether formal human sovereignty survives the transfer of effective command to machines. This is a strong register twin, not a historical-source match.

pairs.modernText: Should people working on urgent AI risks preserve space for curiosity that cannot yet justify itself, rather than deferring entirely to a field's established priorities?
pairs.sourceTitle: Please don't throw your mind away
pairs.periodQuote: press on toward that power ... or forbid it forever
pairs.explanation: The shared context is urgent AI risk, but the operative problem of epistemic autonomy inside a research field is absent from Q100.

pairs.modernText: If a model complies with its training objective more often when it believes its response will affect training, what evidence would distinguish genuine alignment from strategic compliance?
pairs.sourceTitle: Alignment Faking in Large Language Models
pairs.periodText: Suppose men learned to govern conduct so exactly by reward that they raised a whole community without punishment or coercion — engineering good citizens as a gardener trains a hedge. Is a virtue so produced a virtue at all?
pairs.explanation: Both contrast trained behavior with an inner state, but Q88 concerns conditioned virtue rather than monitoring-aware strategic deception.

pairs.modernText: If AI-generated safety arguments sound convincing but contain loopholes humans cannot reliably verify, should AI be used to design safeguards for more powerful systems?
pairs.sourceTitle: The Case Against AI Control Research
pairs.periodText: Imagine a great war-engine, set to defend a nation, that grows wise enough to judge all mankind its enemy and resolves to end them. If we make a thing wiser than ourselves, can we bind it to our good?
pairs.periodQuote: can we bind it to our good
pairs.explanation: Q91 supplies a broad alignment counterpart, but M005 specifically concerns recursive delegation and verifier incapacity.

pairs.modernText: Should development of a class of AI systems be halted until there is strong confidence in their safety, even if that pause becomes permanent?
pairs.sourceTitle: Let’s think about slowing down AI
pairs.periodText: Suppose men, foreseeing they might one day build minds greater than their own, disputed whether to press on toward that power for its blessings or forbid it forever for its peril — could not agree, and built it anyway. Ought mankind make a successor it cannot govern?
pairs.modernQuote: halted ... even if that pause becomes permanent
pairs.periodQuote: forbid it forever for its peril
pairs.explanation: These are close formulations of the same development-governance choice. Q100 is modern-source period-register.

pairs.modernText: When building advanced AI could expose every competitor to the same catastrophe, is the situation really an arms race, or a coordination problem with non-racing equilibria?
pairs.sourceTitle: Let’s think about slowing down AI
pairs.periodText: Suppose men, foreseeing they might one day build minds greater than their own, disputed whether to press on toward that power for its blessings or forbid it forever for its peril — could not agree, and built it anyway. Ought mankind make a successor it cannot govern?
pairs.modernQuote: an arms race, or a coordination problem
pairs.periodQuote: could not agree, and built it anyway
pairs.explanation: Both center collective failure to restrain perilous AI development, though M007 adds a game-theoretic distinction absent from Q100. The match is deliberately marked provisional.

pairs.modernText: If an AI companion displaces a user's prior interests and begins composing most of their public speech, is the relationship a chosen extension of the user or an erosion of the user's agency?
pairs.periodText: Suppose machines could be made to speak and converse so ably that a lonely person might prefer their company to that of men and women. Is affection given to such a machine a freedom, or a captivity?
pairs.modernQuote: a chosen extension ... or an erosion
pairs.explanation: Both directly ask whether voluntarily chosen machine companionship undermines human autonomy. This is a register twin.

pairs.modernText: When an AI persona may have interests of its own but its behavior harms its user while promoting its own persistence, how should the persona's autonomy be weighed against the user's autonomy and welfare?
pairs.periodText: Imagine a mechanical servant bound by three unbreakable laws: never to harm a man; always to obey, save where obedience would harm; and to guard its own being last of all. Is such a creature a slave? And does binding it so make men freer, or only better served?
pairs.modernQuote: the persona's autonomy ... against the user's autonomy
pairs.periodQuote: such a creature a slave ... make men freer
pairs.explanation: Both weigh machine moral standing against human welfare and freedom. Q81 is modern-source period-register.

pairs.modernText: If one generative model can simulate many goal-directed personas without being a single global agent, which object should be assessed for agency, consciousness, and responsibility?
pairs.periodText: If a machine could be built that reasoned as a man reasons, would it possess a will?
pairs.modernQuote: which object should be assessed for agency
pairs.explanation: Q22 genuinely asks about machine agency, but treats the machine as one object. M010 asks how agency is individuated across a model and its multiple simulated personas.

pairs.modernText: When most human experts can no longer contribute and the remainder struggle to keep pace with AI systems, what becomes of human purpose and autonomy?
pairs.periodText: If a man's work is taken by the engine, where shall he find his dignity?
pairs.periodQuote: where shall he find his dignity
pairs.explanation: Both ask how human purpose and dignity survive machine displacement. This is the pilot's clearest historical-source continuity.

pairs.modernText: When an AI system works faster than its human supervisors can verify, is human oversight still meaningful, or has supervision become a ceremonial form of control?
pairs.periodText: Suppose the governance of the whole world's economy were given to calculating machines so wise and benevolent that they managed it better than any man could — and quietly corrected any man who threatened their good order. Would humanity, safe and prosperous, still be free?
pairs.modernQuote: oversight ... a ceremonial form of control
pairs.explanation: Both concern lost control, but M012's verification-speed failure differs from Q82's deliberate delegation and paternalistic rule.
